title: Epsilon-delta definition of a limit
text: The statement lim_{x->a} f(x) = L means that for every epsilon > 0 there exists a delta > 0 such that whenever 0 < |x - a| < delta we have |f(x) - L| < epsilon. This formalizes the idea that f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by taking x sufficiently close to a (but not equal to a). The limit describes the behavior of f near a, not its value at a.

title: The Squeeze Theorem
text: If g(x) <= f(x) <= h(x) for all x near a (except possibly at a) and lim_{x->a} g(x) = lim_{x->a} h(x) = L, then lim_{x->a} f(x) = L. This theorem is used to evaluate limits of functions bounded between two others with equal limits, such as x^2 sin(1/x) near 0 or the standard limit of sin(x)/x as x approaches 0.

title: L'Hopital's rule for indeterminate forms
text: If lim f(x)/g(x) has the indeterminate form 0/0 or infinity/infinity and f, g are differentiable near a with g'(x) nonzero, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x) provided the latter exists. Other indeterminate forms such as 0 times infinity or infinity minus infinity must first be rewritten algebraically as a quotient before the rule applies.

title: Continuity at a point
text: A function f is continuous at a if three conditions hold: f(a) is defined, lim_{x->a} f(x) exists, and lim_{x->a} f(x) = f(a). Discontinuities are classified as removable (a hole where the limit exists but disagrees with or is missing the value), jump (one-sided limits differ), or infinite (a vertical asymptote).

title: Intermediate Value Theorem
text: If f is continuous on the closed interval [a, b] and N is any value strictly between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) with f(c) = N. A common corollary: if f is continuous on [a, b] and f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, then f has a root in (a, b). Continuity on the whole closed interval is essential.

title: Limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
text: The line y = L is a horizontal asymptote of f if lim_{x->infinity} f(x) = L or lim_{x->-infinity} f(x) = L. For a rational function, the end behavior is governed by the leading terms: if numerator and denominator have equal degree the limit is the ratio of leading coefficients, if the denominator degree is larger the limit is 0, and if the numerator degree is larger the function grows without bound.

title: Definition of the derivative
text: The derivative of f at a is f'(a) = lim_{h->0} (f(a+h) - f(a)) / h, when this limit exists. Equivalently it is the limit of the difference quotient (f(x) - f(a))/(x - a) as x approaches a. Geometrically f'(a) is the slope of the tangent line to y = f(x) at the point (a, f(a)), and differentiability at a implies continuity at a.

title: The power rule
text: For any real exponent n, d/dx (x^n) = n x^(n-1). Combined with linearity of the derivative (the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives and constants factor out), the power rule differentiates any polynomial term by term. For example d/dx (3x^4 - 5x) = 12x^3 - 5.

title: Product and quotient rules
text: The product rule states (fg)' = f'g + fg'. The quotient rule states (f/g)' = (f'g - fg') / g^2 wherever g is nonzero. These rules are needed because the derivative does not distribute over products or quotients; for instance d/dx (x^2 sin x) = 2x sin x + x^2 cos x.

title: The chain rule
text: The chain rule differentiates a composition of functions: if y = f(g(x)) then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) g'(x), the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner function times the derivative of the inner function. For example d/dx sin(x^2) = cos(x^2) times 2x. The chain rule underlies implicit differentiation and related-rates problems.

title: Derivatives of trigonometric functions
text: The basic trigonometric derivatives are d/dx sin x = cos x, d/dx cos x = -sin x, and d/dx tan x = sec^2 x. These follow from the definition of the derivative together with the limits sin(h)/h -> 1 and (cos(h) - 1)/h -> 0 as h approaches 0. Combined with the chain rule they differentiate any trigonometric composition.

title: Mean Value Theorem
text: If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists c in (a, b) with f'(c) = (f(b) - f(a)) / (b - a). The special case f(a) = f(b) is Rolle's theorem. A key corollary is that a function with derivative identically zero on an interval is constant there.

title: Critical points and the first derivative test
text: A critical point of f is an interior point where f'(x) = 0 or f' is undefined; by Fermat's theorem all local extrema of a differentiable function occur at critical points. The first derivative test classifies a critical point: f has a local maximum where f' changes from positive to negative and a local minimum where f' changes from negative to positive.

title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
text: Part 1 says that if f is continuous and F(x) = integral from a to x of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x). Part 2 says that if F is any antiderivative of a continuous f on [a, b], then the definite integral from a to b of f(x) dx equals F(b) - F(a). Together they link differentiation and integration as inverse processes.

title: Basic antiderivatives
text: Reversing the power rule gives the integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C for n not equal to -1, while the integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. Other standard forms include the integral of e^x dx = e^x + C and the integral of cos x dx = sin x + C. The arbitrary constant C reflects that antiderivatives are unique only up to an added constant.

title: Substitution rule (u-substitution)
text: The substitution rule reverses the chain rule: the integral of f(g(x)) g'(x) dx equals the integral of f(u) du where u = g(x). One chooses u so that its differential du = g'(x) dx appears in the integrand. For definite integrals the limits must be converted to the new variable u.

title: Integration by parts
text: Integration by parts states the integral of u dv equals uv minus the integral of v du, obtained by reversing the product rule for derivatives. It is effective when the integrand is a product such as x e^x or x ln x, and a good choice of u simplifies the remaining integral. Repeated application handles integrands like x^2 sin x.

title: Area between curves
text: The area of the region bounded by y = f(x) above and y = g(x) below over [a, b] is the integral from a to b of (f(x) - g(x)) dx, provided f(x) >= g(x) throughout. When the curves cross, the interval must be split at the intersection points so each subintegral integrates the larger minus the smaller function.

title: Improper integrals
text: An improper integral over an unbounded interval is defined as a limit, for example the integral from 1 to infinity of f(x) dx equals the limit as t -> infinity of the integral from 1 to t of f(x) dx. It converges if the limit is a finite number and diverges otherwise; the p-integral of 1/x^p from 1 to infinity converges exactly when p > 1.

title: Average value and the Mean Value Theorem for integrals
text: The average value of a continuous function f on [a, b] is (1/(b - a)) times the integral from a to b of f(x) dx. The Mean Value Theorem for integrals guarantees a point c in [a, b] at which f(c) equals this average value, so f actually attains its mean somewhere on the interval.

title: Geometric series
text: A geometric series sum of a r^n from n = 0 converges if and only if |r| < 1, and then its sum is a / (1 - r). If |r| >= 1 the series diverges. Geometric series are the benchmark against which comparison tests are often applied.

title: The ratio test
text: For a series sum a_n, let L be the limit of |a_{n+1} / a_n| as n -> infinity. The series converges absolutely if L < 1, diverges if L > 1, and the test is inconclusive if L = 1. The ratio test is especially effective for series involving factorials or exponentials.

title: p-series and the integral test
text: The p-series sum of 1/n^p converges if and only if p > 1 and diverges for p <= 1; in particular the harmonic series (p = 1) diverges. This is established by the integral test, which compares a series with positive decreasing terms to the improper integral of the corresponding continuous function.

title: Alternating series test
text: An alternating series sum of (-1)^n b_n with b_n > 0 converges if the terms b_n decrease monotonically to 0. Such a series may converge conditionally without converging absolutely, as with the alternating harmonic series. The error of a partial sum is bounded by the first omitted term.

title: Taylor and Maclaurin series
text: The Taylor series of f about a is the sum of f^(n)(a)/n! times (x - a)^n. The Maclaurin series is the special case a = 0. Standard Maclaurin series include e^x = sum x^n/n!, sin x = sum (-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, and 1/(1 - x) = sum x^n for |x| < 1.

title: Power series and radius of convergence
text: A power series sum c_n (x - a)^n converges on an interval centered at a. Its radius of convergence R, often found via the ratio test, gives convergence for |x - a| < R and divergence for |x - a| > R; the endpoints must be checked separately. Within the open interval the series may be differentiated and integrated term by term.

title: The divergence test
text: If the limit of a_n as n -> infinity is not zero (or fails to exist), then the series sum a_n diverges. This nth-term test can only prove divergence: a_n -> 0 is necessary but not sufficient for convergence, as the divergent harmonic series shows.

title: Partial derivatives
text: The partial derivative of f(x, y) with respect to x is obtained by differentiating with respect to x while holding y constant, and similarly for y. It measures the instantaneous rate of change of f along one coordinate direction. Higher-order partials are formed by repeated partial differentiation.

title: The gradient vector
text: The gradient of f(x, y) is the vector grad f = (df/dx, df/dy) of its partial derivatives. It points in the direction of greatest increase of f, its magnitude is the maximum rate of increase, and it is orthogonal to the level curves of f. The directional derivative in a unit direction u equals the dot product of grad f with u.

title: Clairaut's theorem on mixed partials
text: If the mixed second-order partial derivatives f_xy and f_yx are both continuous on a disk containing a point, then they are equal at that point: f_xy = f_yx. Thus for well-behaved functions the order of partial differentiation does not matter.

title: Critical points and the second derivative test
text: A critical point of f(x, y) occurs where grad f = 0 or a partial is undefined. The second derivative test uses the discriminant D = f_xx f_yy - (f_xy)^2: if D > 0 and f_xx > 0 there is a local minimum, if D > 0 and f_xx < 0 a local maximum, and if D < 0 a saddle point; D = 0 is inconclusive.

title: Double integrals and Fubini's theorem
text: The double integral of f(x, y) over a region gives the signed volume under the surface z = f(x, y). Over a rectangle, Fubini's theorem lets it be evaluated as an iterated integral, integrating first with respect to one variable and then the other; for continuous f the order of integration may be exchanged.

title: Multivariable chain rule
text: If z = f(x, y) with x and y functions of t, then dz/dt = (df/dx)(dx/dt) + (df/dy)(dy/dt). More generally, when the independent variables themselves depend on several parameters, the partial derivatives combine as a sum of products over each intermediate variable.

title: Lagrange multipliers
text: To extremize f(x, y) subject to a constraint g(x, y) = c, solve grad f = lambda grad g together with the constraint. At a constrained extremum the gradients of the objective and constraint are parallel, and lambda is the Lagrange multiplier. The method extends to functions of more variables and multiple constraints.

title: Definition of a vector space
text: A vector space over a field is a set closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication and satisfying eight axioms: associativity and commutativity of addition, an additive identity (zero vector) and additive inverses, and the distributive, associative, and identity laws for scalar multiplication. Examples include R^n, spaces of matrices, and spaces of polynomials.
source_citation: Hefferon, Linear Algebra (CC BY-SA / free license), Ch. 1

title: Linear independence
text: A set of vectors is linearly independent if the only solution to c_1 v_1 + ... + c_k v_k = 0 is the trivial one c_1 = ... = c_k = 0. Otherwise the set is dependent and some vector is a linear combination of the others. Independence guarantees that each vector in a span has a unique representation.

title: Basis of a vector space
text: A basis of a vector space is a linearly independent set that spans the space, so every vector is a unique linear combination of the basis vectors. The standard basis of R^n consists of the coordinate vectors e_1, ..., e_n. Every vector space has a basis, and any two bases have the same number of elements.

title: Dimension
text: The dimension of a vector space is the number of vectors in any basis, a well-defined quantity because all bases have equal size. In an n-dimensional space, any set of more than n vectors is dependent and any independent set of exactly n vectors is automatically a basis. The dimension of R^n is n.

title: Subspaces
text: A subset W of a vector space is a subspace if it is nonempty and closed under addition and scalar multiplication; equivalently it must contain the zero vector and every linear combination of its elements. The span of any set of vectors, the null space of a matrix, and the column space of a matrix are all subspaces.

title: Span of a set of vectors
text: The span of a set of vectors is the set of all their linear combinations, and it is the smallest subspace containing them. A set spans a space when every vector of the space is some linear combination of the set. A spanning set that is also linearly independent is a basis.

title: Determinant and invertibility
text: The determinant of a 2x2 matrix [[a, b], [c, d]] is ad - bc. A square matrix A is invertible if and only if det(A) is nonzero; equivalently its columns are linearly independent, its rows are linearly independent, and the system Ax = 0 has only the trivial solution. The determinant of a product is the product of determinants.

title: Matrix multiplication
text: The product AB is defined when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B; the (i, j) entry of AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B. Matrix multiplication is associative and distributes over addition but is not commutative in general. It represents composition of the associated linear maps.

title: Transpose and symmetric matrices
text: The transpose A^T of a matrix satisfies (A^T)_{ij} = A_{ji}, swapping rows and columns; it obeys (AB)^T = B^T A^T. A matrix is symmetric when A = A^T and skew-symmetric when A^T = -A. Real symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.

title: Rank and row reduction
text: The rank of a matrix is the number of nonzero rows in its reduced row echelon form, equal to the dimension of its column space and of its row space. Row reduction (Gaussian elimination) computes the rank and solves linear systems; a system Ax = b is consistent exactly when the rank of A equals the rank of the augmented matrix.

title: Matrix inverse
text: The inverse of a square matrix A is the matrix A^{-1} with A A^{-1} = A^{-1} A = I. It exists precisely when det(A) is nonzero and can be computed by row-reducing the augmented matrix [A | I] to [I | A^{-1}]. For a 2x2 matrix the inverse is (1/det) times [[d, -b], [-c, a]].

title: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
text: A scalar lambda is an eigenvalue of a square matrix A if there is a nonzero vector v (an eigenvector) with A v = lambda v. Equivalently lambda satisfies det(A - lambda I) = 0. The eigenvectors for a fixed lambda, together with the zero vector, form the eigenspace, which is the null space of A - lambda I.

title: Characteristic polynomial
text: The characteristic polynomial of A is p(lambda) = det(A - lambda I); its roots are exactly the eigenvalues of A, counted with algebraic multiplicity. For an n x n matrix it has degree n. Finding eigenvalues therefore reduces to computing this determinant and solving the resulting polynomial equation.

title: Diagonalization
text: An n x n matrix A is diagonalizable if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors, in which case A = P D P^{-1} where D is diagonal with the eigenvalues and the columns of P are the corresponding eigenvectors. A matrix with n distinct eigenvalues is always diagonalizable. Diagonalization makes computing powers A^k straightforward.

title: Trace and determinant via eigenvalues
text: For a square matrix, the trace (sum of diagonal entries) equals the sum of the eigenvalues, and the determinant equals the product of the eigenvalues, each counted with algebraic multiplicity. These relations follow from the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial and give quick checks on computed eigenvalues.

title: Spectral theorem for symmetric matrices
text: A real symmetric matrix has only real eigenvalues and can be orthogonally diagonalized: there is an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors, so A = Q D Q^T with Q orthogonal and D diagonal. Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are automatically orthogonal.

title: Definition of a linear map
text: A map T from V to W is linear if T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(c v) = c T(v) for all vectors u, v and scalars c; equivalently it preserves linear combinations. Every linear map from R^n to R^m is given by multiplication by an m by n matrix, and composition of linear maps corresponds to matrix multiplication.

title: Kernel of a linear map
text: The kernel (null space) of a linear map T is the set of vectors v with T(v) = 0; it is a subspace of the domain. A linear map is injective (one-to-one) if and only if its kernel contains only the zero vector. For a matrix, the kernel is the solution set of the homogeneous system Ax = 0.

title: Image of a linear map
text: The image (range) of a linear map T from V to W is the set of all outputs T(v), a subspace of W. For a matrix it equals the column space, the span of the columns. A linear map is surjective (onto) exactly when its image is all of the codomain.

title: Rank-nullity theorem
text: For a linear map T from a finite-dimensional space V to W, the dimension of the kernel plus the dimension of the image equals the dimension of V: dim(ker T) + dim(im T) = dim V. The dimension of the image is the rank and the dimension of the kernel is the nullity. This constrains when a map can be injective or surjective.

title: Isomorphisms of vector spaces
text: An isomorphism is a linear map that is both injective and surjective (a bijection); it has a linear inverse. Two finite-dimensional vector spaces are isomorphic if and only if they have the same dimension, so every n-dimensional real space is isomorphic to R^n via a choice of basis.

title: Matrix representation of a linear map
text: Given bases for the domain and codomain, a linear map is represented by a matrix whose columns are the coordinate vectors of the images of the domain basis vectors. Changing bases replaces the matrix M by a similar matrix P^{-1} M P via a change-of-basis matrix. This is how abstract linear maps become concrete matrix computations.

title: Column space of a matrix
text: The column space of an m by n matrix A is the span of its columns, a subspace of R^m consisting of every vector of the form A x. The system A x = b is solvable exactly when b lies in the column space. Its dimension is the rank of A.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 6: Column space and nullspace

title: Null space of a matrix
text: The null space of an m by n matrix A is the set of all solutions x to the homogeneous system A x = 0, a subspace of R^n. It is closed under linear combinations because A is linear. Its dimension is the number of free (non-pivot) columns, and it contains only the zero vector precisely when the columns of A are independent.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 6: Column space and nullspace

title: Coordinates relative to a basis
text: Once a basis B = (b_1, ..., b_n) of a vector space is fixed, every vector v has a unique list of coordinates (c_1, ..., c_n) with v = c_1 b_1 + ... + c_n b_n. Uniqueness follows from linear independence of the basis. These coordinate vectors give an isomorphism between the space and R^n.

title: Extending an independent set to a basis
text: In a finite-dimensional space, any linearly independent set can be extended to a basis, and any spanning set can be reduced to a basis. Consequently an independent set never has more vectors than a spanning set, and both a maximal independent set and a minimal spanning set have exactly the dimension of the space.

title: Independence, basis, and dimension
text: Vectors are independent when no nontrivial combination gives the zero vector; a basis is an independent set that spans the space; and the dimension is the common size of every basis. For the column space and null space of a matrix these dimensions are the rank and the nullity, which add up to the number of columns.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 9: Independence, basis, and dimension

title: Sums and intersections of subspaces
text: If U and W are subspaces of a vector space, their intersection and their sum U + W (all u + w) are again subspaces. Their dimensions satisfy dim(U + W) = dim U + dim W - dim(U intersect W). The sum is called direct when U intersect W is just the zero vector, and then every vector of U + W splits uniquely as u + w.

title: Properties of determinants
text: The determinant is the unique function of a square matrix that is linear in each row, changes sign when two rows are swapped, and equals 1 for the identity. From these follow that a matrix with a zero row or two equal rows has determinant 0, that adding a multiple of one row to another leaves the determinant unchanged, and that det(A B) = det(A) det(B).
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 18: Properties of determinants

title: Cofactor (Laplace) expansion of a determinant
text: The determinant of an n by n matrix can be expanded along any row or column as a sum of entries times their cofactors, where the cofactor C_ij = (-1)^(i+j) times the minor obtained by deleting row i and column j. For a 3x3 matrix expanding along the first row gives a(ei - fh) - b(di - fg) + c(dh - eg).
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 19: Determinant formulas and cofactors

title: Cramer's rule and the inverse via cofactors
text: For an invertible A, Cramer's rule gives each unknown as x_i = det(A_i) / det(A), where A_i is A with its i-th column replaced by b. Equivalently the inverse is A^{-1} = (1/det A) times the adjugate (the transpose of the cofactor matrix). These formulas are mainly theoretical; row reduction is far more efficient in practice.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 20: Cramer's rule, inverse matrix, and volume

title: Computing an inverse by Gauss-Jordan elimination
text: To invert a square matrix A, augment it with the identity to form [A | I] and row-reduce until the left block becomes I; the right block is then A^{-1}. This succeeds exactly when A has full rank (a pivot in every column). It is the Gauss-Jordan method and simultaneously solves A x = e_j for each standard basis vector.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 3: Multiplication and inverse matrices

title: Elementary row operations and echelon form
text: Gauss's method uses three row operations: swapping two rows, scaling a row by a nonzero constant, and adding a multiple of one row to another. Each operation preserves the solution set of a linear system and corresponds to left multiplication by an elementary matrix. Repeated application produces (reduced) row echelon form, from which rank and solutions are read off.

title: Equivalent conditions for invertibility
text: For a square n by n matrix A the following are equivalent: A is invertible; det(A) is nonzero; A has rank n; the columns of A are linearly independent; the rows are independent; A x = 0 has only the trivial solution; and A x = b has a unique solution for every b. Any one of these conditions implies all the others.

title: Rank, pivots, and free variables
text: The rank r of a matrix equals its number of pivots in row echelon form; the r pivot columns give a basis for the column space. An m by n matrix then has n - r free variables, so the null space has dimension n - r. Full column rank (r = n) means a unique solution or none; full row rank (r = m) means a solution always exists.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 8: Solving Ax = b: row reduced form R

title: Finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors
text: An eigenvector x of A satisfies A x = lambda x, so it keeps its direction under A and is merely scaled by the eigenvalue lambda. The eigenvalues are the roots of det(A - lambda I) = 0; for each one the eigenvectors are the nonzero solutions of (A - lambda I) x = 0. A 2x2 example: for [[2,1],[1,2]] the eigenvalues are 3 and 1 with eigenvectors (1,1) and (1,-1).
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 21: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors

title: Diagonalization and powers of a matrix
text: If A has n independent eigenvectors, form S with those eigenvectors as columns and the diagonal matrix Lambda of eigenvalues; then A = S Lambda S^{-1}. Powers become easy: A^k = S Lambda^k S^{-1}, and Lambda^k just raises each eigenvalue to the k-th power. This is why diagonalization governs the long-run behavior of repeated matrix multiplication.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 22: Diagonalization and powers of A

title: Algebraic and geometric multiplicity
text: The algebraic multiplicity of an eigenvalue is its multiplicity as a root of the characteristic polynomial; its geometric multiplicity is the dimension of the eigenspace (the null space of A - lambda I). The geometric multiplicity never exceeds the algebraic one. A matrix is diagonalizable exactly when the two multiplicities agree for every eigenvalue.

title: Similar matrices share eigenvalues
text: Two square matrices A and B are similar if B = P^{-1} A P for some invertible P; similarity means they represent the same linear map in different bases. Similar matrices have the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues with the same algebraic multiplicities, and equal trace and determinant. Diagonalization is the special case where B is diagonal.

title: Eigenvalues of symmetric matrices
text: A real symmetric matrix has only real eigenvalues and a full set of orthogonal eigenvectors, so it can be written A = Q Lambda Q^T with Q orthogonal. The number of positive eigenvalues equals the number of positive pivots. This spectral decomposition underlies principal axes and positive-definiteness tests.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 25: Symmetric matrices and positive definiteness

title: Eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix from trace and determinant
text: For a 2x2 matrix the characteristic polynomial is lambda^2 - (trace) lambda + (det), so the eigenvalues solve lambda^2 - (a+d) lambda + (ad - bc) = 0. Their sum is the trace a + d and their product is the determinant ad - bc. This gives a quick route to both eigenvalues without expanding a larger determinant.

title: Matrix of a linear transformation
text: A linear transformation T is determined by its action on a basis: the matrix of T (with respect to chosen input and output bases) has as its j-th column the coordinates of T applied to the j-th input basis vector. Then applying T becomes multiplying by that matrix. Rotations, projections, and reflections of the plane are standard examples.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 30: Linear transformations and their matrices

title: Change of basis and similar matrices
text: Changing the basis replaces a coordinate vector by M c, where M is the invertible change-of-basis matrix. The matrix of a fixed linear operator in a new basis is M^{-1} A M, a matrix similar to A. Choosing a basis of eigenvectors makes this new matrix diagonal, which is exactly diagonalization.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 31: Change of basis; image compression

title: The four fundamental subspaces
text: Every m by n matrix A has four fundamental subspaces: the column space and left null space live in R^m, while the row space and null space live in R^n. The column space and row space both have dimension r (the rank); the null space has dimension n - r and the left null space has dimension m - r. The row space and null space are orthogonal complements in R^n.
source_citation: MIT OCW 18.06, Linear Algebra (Strang), Lecture 10: The four fundamental subspaces

title: Composition of linear maps is matrix multiplication
text: If S and T are linear maps, their composition S after T is again linear, and the matrix of the composition is the product of the individual matrices in the same order. This is precisely why matrix multiplication is defined by the row-times-column rule, and why it is associative but not generally commutative.
source_citation: Hefferon, Linear Algebra, Ch. 3 (Maps Between Spaces)

title: Injectivity, surjectivity, and dimension
text: A linear map is injective exactly when its kernel is trivial, and surjective exactly when its image is the whole codomain. By rank-nullity, a linear map between spaces of the same finite dimension is injective if and only if it is surjective, so for square matrices one-to-one and onto coincide. Between spaces of unequal dimension one direction must fail.
source_citation: Hefferon, Linear Algebra, Ch. 3 (Maps Between Spaces)

title: Rank of a linear map
text: The rank of a linear map is the dimension of its image, equal to the rank of any matrix that represents it. Rank-nullity then says the domain dimension is the rank plus the nullity. A map from an n-dimensional space has rank at most n, and reaching that maximum means the map is injective.
source_citation: Hefferon, Linear Algebra, Ch. 3 (Maps Between Spaces)

title: Invertible linear maps
text: A linear map is invertible when it is a bijection, and its inverse is automatically linear. For a map represented by a square matrix A this happens precisely when A is invertible, and the inverse map is represented by A^{-1}. An invertible linear map carries any basis of the domain to a basis of the codomain.
source_citation: Hefferon, Linear Algebra, Ch. 3 (Maps Between Spaces)
